coredata.dc:title: Infrared-assisted synthesis of highly amidized graphene quantum dots as metal-free electrochemical catalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Siyong
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Mallick, Bikash Chandra
Gandomi Ashraf, Yasser
coredata.dc:description: Highly amidized graphene quantum dot (GQD) catalysts are successfully synthesized and employed as metal-free electrocatalyst for electrochemical hydrogen adsorption and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic and alkaline electrolytes. The GQD catalysts are prepared through an efficient IR-assisted technique involving the pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 250 °C. The as-prepared GQDs with an average particle size of 30 nm contain a high amidation level (N/C atomic ratio: 23‒46 at.%) with a large amount of O-rich functionalities. The highly amidized GQD catalytic electrodes provide ultra-high electrochemically active surface area toward H-adsorption with superior durability upon extended cycling (> 400 cycles). The GQD electrodes demonstrate an onset potential appearing at ca. -0.17 V with a well-defined reduction peak at ca. -0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl in KOH electrolyte. The ORR catalytic activity in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes is significantly enhanced via N-doping into the GQDs nanostructure. Two ORR mechanisms are proposed to elaborate the sequential reduction pathways within acid and base electrolytes, including chemsorption of O2, four-electron reduction route, and two-electron charge transfer pathway. Therefore, the design of amidized GQDs paves the pathway towards developing highly efficient metal-free catalysts for electrochemical H-storage and ORR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen reduction reaction
Electrochemical hydrogen adsorption
Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen doping
Metal-free catalysts

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: IR-assisted synthesis of highly amidized GQDs
Electrochemical evaluation of highly amidized gqd electrodes
Spectroscopic and material characterization of highly amidized gqd samples
Materials characterization of highly amidized gqd catalysts
ESA of highly amidized gqd catalyst electrodes
ORR activity of highly amidized gqd catalyst electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs and (b) PL spectra of different GQD suspensions under 360-nm UV irradiation.

UV absorption spectra of different aqueous GQD suspensions: (a) LN-GQD and (b) HN-GQD. (c) Raman spectra and (d) XRD patterns of different GQD samples. In Figs. 2(a) and 2(b), the blue arrows indicate C = C, C = O, and surface molecular center or non-bonding electrons of adatoms (from low to high wavelengths).
XPS O 1 s and N 1 s peak of different GQD samples: (a,d) LN-GQD, (b,e) MN-GQD, and (c,f) HN-GQD, deconvoluted by a multiple Gaussian function.
HR-TEM micrographs of GQD samples: (a) LN-GQD, (b) MN-GQD, and (c) HN-GQD.FE-SEM images of different GQD-coated CP electrodes: (d) LN-GQD, (e) MN-GQD, and (f) HN-GQD.

(a) Typical CV profiles of MN-GQD catalyst electrode in 1 M sulfuric acid under nitrogen atmospheres at different scan rates and (b) the variation of ESA with scan rate for various samples. (c) CV profiles of MN-GQD catalyst electrode at 50 mV s −1 for 400 cycles and (d) the variation of ESA with cycle number for different electrodes.
Schematic of hydrogen adsorption on highly amidized GQDs, where the carbon atoms next to pyridinic N and graphitic N atoms serve as favorable H-adsorption sites.

Typical LSV profiles of different GQD/CP catalyst electrodes in different electrolytes under O2 atmosphere: (a) LN-GQD, (b) MN-GQD, and (c) HN-GQD in 1 M H2SO4, and (d) LN-GQD, (e) MN-GQD, and (f) HN-GQD in 0.1 M KOH. The scan rates were set at 10, 30, 50, and 100 mV s −1.

Schematic of ORR on amidized GQDs with (a) low and (b) high amidiation level, where the carbon atoms next to pyridinic N and edge-N oxide groups serve as active sites for ORR in 4-electron and 2-electron pathway, respectively.

head.ce:title: Infrared-assisted synthesis of highly amidized graphene quantum dots as metal-free electrochemical catalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical, Aerospace, and Biomedical Engineering, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN , United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Mechanical, Aerospace, and Biomedical Engineering, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN , United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA , United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Chemical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA , United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly amidized graphene quantum dot (GQD) catalysts are successfully synthesized and employed as metal-free electrocatalyst for electrochemical hydrogen adsorption and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic and alkaline electrolytes. The GQD catalysts are prepared through an efficient IR-assisted technique involving the pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 250 °C. The as-prepared GQDs with an average particle size of 30 nm contain a high amidation level (N/C atomic ratio: 23‒46 at.%) with a large amount of O-rich functionalities. The highly amidized GQD catalytic electrodes provide ultra-high electrochemically active surface area toward H-adsorption with superior durability upon extended cycling (> 400 cycles). The GQD electrodes demonstrate an onset potential appearing at ca. -0.17 V with a well-defined reduction peak at ca. -0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl in KOH electrolyte. The ORR catalytic activity in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes is significantly enhanced via N-doping into the GQDs nanostructure. Two ORR mechanisms are proposed to elaborate the sequential reduction pathways within acid and base electrolytes, including chemsorption of O2, four-electron reduction route, and two-electron charge transfer pathway. Therefore, the design of amidized GQDs paves the pathway towards developing highly efficient metal-free catalysts for electrochemical H-storage and ORR.

ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) occurring on the high-potential electrode (i.e., cathode) is employed within several electrochemical systems (e.g., fuel cells, metal-air batteries) for the energy storage/conversion [1]. Commonly, carbon-supported Pt or Pt-based alloys serve as the electrocatalyst in these systems [2,3]. Although Pt-based nanoparticles are recognized as the most efficient catalysts for ORR [4,5], the commercialization of Pt-based electrocatalysts is hindered by high cost, scarcity, and poor long-term durability [6–8]. In particular, several prior studies have reported on the susceptibility of the Pt-based catalysts to CO poisoning [9,10]. Therefore, developing highly efficient yet inexpensive catalyst for the ORR is of significant importance.

Extensive previous efforts have been devoted for developing non-precious metal [11,12] and metal-free catalysts [13–15] for the ORR activity. Recently, heteroatom (mostly nitrogen, N)-doped carbon nanostructures have been emerged as highly efficeint electrocatalysts due to superior activity toward ORR within the alkaline electrolytes. Other major advantages of these catalysts include high stability, and remarkable resistivity toward CO or methanol poisoning [1,10,16–18].

The incorporation of N atoms within the carbon network based on the nitrogen atoms’ bonding configuration can be classified into two categories: (i) chemical N atoms such as bonding to O atoms (oxidized nitrogen species) and (ii) lattice N atoms including six-membered rings (pyridinic N), five-membered rings (pyrrolic N), and graphene basal plane (graphitic/quaternary-N center) [19–22]. The role of different N-containing functionalities (e.g., pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N) as the catalytic sites toward ORR has been explored through theoretical calculations and experimental measurements. These studies highlight the importance of amidation level on the carbon electrodes for improving the ORR activity.

Several approaches have been adopted for synthesizing N-doped carbon nanostructures, including a hydrothermal route using NH4OH as the nitrogen source [23], chemical vapor deposition with pyridine as the source of both C and N [24], and pyrolysis of citric acid and ethanolamine precursors [25]. Despite being promising, the N/C atomic ratio on the N-doped carbon structures achieved using these techniques had only been in the range of 5‒15 at.% majorly due to the formation of pyridinic or pyrrolic N atoms [23]. Therefore, there is a significant need for preparing highly amidized carbon catalyst electrodes to improve the ORR efficiency.

We have already demonstrated synthesis routes for preparing highly amidized and oxidized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through the pyrolysis of citric acid and urea under microwave- and infrared (IR) irradiation [26,27]. The GQDs can be considered as highly efficient catalysts for ORR due to remarkable amidation level, i.e., high N/C ratio: 22‒50 at.%. Being a promising catalyst for the ORR, it is also critical to explore the electrochemical evaluation of ORR activity on highly amidized/oxidized GQD electrodes. In this work, we have synthesized three types of GQDs with low, medium, and high amidation level through an IR-assisted synthesis technique. Herein we have explored the distribution of N-functionalities on the ORR activity of GQD electrodes in H2SO4 and KOH electrolytes. The finding of this study paves the pathway for engineering N-doped GQDs as highly efficient and robust metal-free catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IR-assisted synthesis of highly amidized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for growing highly amidized GQDs using the IR-assisted method was reported in our previous study [28]. Adopting the same technique, two precursors, citric acid (C6H8O7, molecular weight: 180, purity: 99%) and urea (CON2H4, molecular weight: 60, purity: 99%), were uniformly mixed using a 2D mixer (with Zr balls) at 300 rpm for 10 min. To prepare N-doped GQDs with different amidation levels, three solid mixtures with varying urea to citric acid molar ratios of 1:1 (labeled as MN), 3:1 (HN), and 1:3 (LN), were prepared. The solid mixtures (10 g) were placed in a homemade IR furnace equipped with six medium-wave IR heater with the near-IR wavelength of 1.4 − 3.2 μm. The solid mixtures were heated to 250 °C in air at heating rate of 6 °C/min and subsequently were maintained at this temperature for 0.5 h. Upon cooling down to the ambient temperature, the GQD samples were moderately ball-milled and sieved through a stainless foil mesh (Type: 200 mesh). Followingly, all the samples were individually dispersed in distilled water and finally centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evaluation of highly amidized gqd electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: For further commercialization, the electrodes were prepared by depositing the GQD samples onto a commercially available carbon paper (CP, Toray Composites Inc.). The GQD-coated CP electrodes in this work closely approached to real electrode assembly of fuel cells, e.g., direct methanol fuel cell and proton exchange membrane fuel cell. The CP substrate was composed of a series of carbon fibers arranged in an irregular configuration with the average fiber diameter of approximately 8–10 μm. Herein 100 mg of the GQD powder was dispersed in a solution containing 30 mL of distilled water and 3 mL of Nafion 117 solution (Aldrich; 5 vol%). The GQD slurry was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 0.5 h to ensure the formation of a uniform paste. A sequential dipping (layer-by-layer) method was employed to deposit the GQDs onto CP substrate with an area of 2 × 1 cm2. Afterward, the GQD-coated CP electrodes were dehydrated at 80 °C in a vacuum oven overnight.

The electrochemical measurement of the GQD-coated CP electrodes was conducted in a three-electrode configuration setup. A Pt wire and an Ag/AgCl electrode (in 3 M KCl aqueous solution) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. First, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was adopted to analyze the catalytic activity of N-doped GQD catalysts in sulfuric acid. The CV measurements were carried out under N2 atmosphere at ambient temperature. The as-prepared electrodes were employed as the working electrode with the projected geometric surface area of 2 × 1 cm2 submerging within the electrolyte. To synthesize the catalytic electrodes, the samples were placed onto a stainless steel foil serving as the current collector. To examine the electrochemical surface area (ESA), the CV measurement of the catalytic electrodes was assessed within the potential range of 0 − 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl using 1 M H2SO4 as the electrolyte. The ESA value, based on the specific charge transfer, contributed from the catalysts (Q H), can be assessed using the following equation [29].
(1)
E S A =
Q H
/ 210 · m

Here, m is the catalyst loading in units of g cm−2, and the factor 210, in units of μC cm−2, was obtained from electrical charge associated with the monolayer adsorption of hydrogen atoms on Pt [29]. The ESA values of the N-doped GQD catalysts were evaluated at various potential scan rates (10, 30, 50, and 100 mV s −1). To explore the durability of the GQD catalysts, the CV measurements were conducted at 30 mV s −1 for 400 cycles. The CV measurement for each electrode was performed repeatedly. The deviation for evaluating electrochemical surface area was within 5.7% for each electrode. To inspect the ORR activity of GQD catalysts, a linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed in 1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M KOH with an O2 gas flowing at volumetric flow rate of 100 ml min−1. Prior to the LSV measurements, high-purity oxygen was introduced into the three-electrode configuration system for 0.5 h. All LSV measurements were performed at the ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and material characterization of highly amidized gqd samples
ce:section.ce:para: An ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrometer (Agilent Technology Cary 60) was employed to analyze the absorbance spectra of N-doped GQD suspensions. The spectroscopic samples were prepared at concentration of 200 mg L −1. The quartz cells were used for UV–Vis spectroscopy at scan rate of 60 nm min−1. A fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P) was adopted to analyze the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra at 360 nm. An X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu Labx XRD-6000), equipped with a Cu‒Kα emitter, was employed to characterize the crystallinity of GQD samples. The bonding structure of GQD samples was analyzed using the Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Micro-Raman spectrometer). The chemical composition of the samples was further examined using XPS (Fison VG ESCA210). The C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s spectra were deconvoluted using the non-linear least squares fitting algorithm with a symmetric Gaussian function. The structural morphology of the GQD-coated CP electrodes was characterized with a field-emission scanning electron spectroscope (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-5600) along with a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials characterization of highly amidized gqd catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 (a) shows photographs of different GQD suspensions under UV illumination at wavelength of 360 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the GQD samples emit different lights: deep blue, deep green, and light green, imparting a red-shift behavior. The PL emission spectra of GQD suspensions excited at 360 nm are shown in Figs. 1(b). Both LN-GQD and MN-GQD samples display an asymmetric peak ranging from 400 to 600 nm under 360-nm excitation. The strongest PL intensities of LN-GQD and MN-GQD samples appear at ca. 435 (2.85 eV) and 475 nm (2.61 eV), respectively. In contrast, HN-GQD sample displays a twin PL peak under 360-nm excitation, at ca. 400 nm (3.10 eV) and 510 nm (2.43 eV). Therefore, the PL emission characteristics of the GQD samples are controlled by the particle size, chemical composition, and surface heterogeneity.

Figs. 2 (a) and 2(b) demonstrate UV–Vis spectra of LN-GQD and HN-GQD suspensions, where three major absorption bands can be identified within the wavelength range of 200‒500 nm. The absorption bands at ca. 200‒250 nm, 300‒350 nm, and 400‒450 nm can be assigned to (i) π→π* transition of C = C of sp2 domain in sp3 C matrix, (ii) n→π* transition of C = O, and (iii) surface molecular center or an absorption edge induced by the (n→π*) transitions of non-bonding electrons of adatoms, respectively [30–33]. It is important to note that the HN-GQD sample possesses an additional band at ~260 nm, which indicates the presence of other species in HN-GQD sample as compared to other electrodes. A similar observation has also been reported in our previous study [28], where the additional band was ascribed to the presence of cyanuric acid (i.e., (CNOH)3) [34].

To further inspect the GQD structures, typical Raman spectra of GQD samples are illustrated in Fig. 2(c). The Raman bands taking place at ca. 1310‒1350 cm−1 and 1560‒1590 cm−1, originate from amorphous carbon or deformation vibrations of a hexagonal ring (D band) and single crystallite of graphite (G band), respectively [35]. The intensity ratio of D to G band can be used to assess the graphitic degree of carbon-based materials. The comparison of the intensity ratio of D to G band among various samples results in the following order: LN-GQD (0.98) > MN-GQD (0.94) > HN-GQD (0.85). Such a trend in the graphitic degree among the samples is primarily due to the existence of N and O decorations or N doping, the vibration of carbon atoms with dangling bonds within the crystal lattice plane, terminations of disordered graphite, and the defects in curved graphene sheets [36].

Fig. 2(d) shows the XRD patterns of the crystalline structure of GQD samples. As demonstrated in Fig. 2(d), one typical d (002) major peak taking place at ca. 26.5‒27.2° (JCPDS Card No.: 41–1487), reveals the presence of crystalline graphite-like structure. Employing the Bragg's Law, the interlayer spacing distances of as-prepared GQDs are in the range of 0.328–0.340 nm, approaching to that of a perfect graphite (i.e., 0.335‒0.340 nm) [37]. As compared to other samples, the HN-GQD sample contains two extra crystalline peaks appearing at 19.7 and 29.8° These two peaks can be attributed to the (111) and (202) crystalline planes of the cyanuric acid (JCPDS Card No.: 23–1637) [28], confirming the insight gained through the analysis of UV–vis spectra. Accordingly, the thermal pyrolysis under IR irradiation tends to grow GQDs accompanying with cyanuric acid at high urea content, inducing a binary crystalline nanostructure of graphene and cyanuric acid.

XPS was adopted to further investigate the chemical composition of highly amidized GQD samples. The N/C atomic ratio of HN-GQD, MN-GQD, and LN-GQD samples were assessed as 46, 33, and 23 at.%, respectively; whereas the O/C ratio of HN-GQD, MN-GQD, and LN-GQD samples follows a similar trend (i.e., 42, 35, and 28 at.%, respectively). Therefore, the as-prepared GQD samples can be considered as highly amidized carbon nanostructure (N/C ratio: 23‒46 at.%) with O-rich functionalities (O/C ratio: 28‒42 at.%), as compared to the N-doped carbon nanostructures prepared using other techniques; e.g., chemical vapor deposition growth of graphene and post-doping with a solid nitrogen precursor of graphitic C3N4 at 700‒900 °C (N content: 6.3 at.%) [11], and chemical impregnation of graphene in 25% NH3•H2O followed by post-heating at 90 °C for 12 h (N content: 5.7‒6.8 at.%) [1].

As shown in Figs. 3 (a)−3(c), the C 1 s spectra of all GQD samples were decomposed into five peaks centered at ca. 284.5 eV (C = C/C‒C), 285.7 eV (C‒N), 286.8 eV (C‒OH), 288.5 eV (C = O), 289.8 eV (O‒C = O), and 291.1 eV (π→π*), respectively, to identify various types of C-bonding. According to Figs. 3, all the GQD samples contain a large number of oxygen functionalities, mainly contributed from the carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups attached to the basal planes or edges of GQDs. Of note, the intensity of the C‒N peak increases with increased urea content, originated from the formation of cyanuric acid or N dopants. To further clarify this point, the asymmetric N 1 s peak was deconvoluted into elemental signals for the pyrrolic/pyridinic N at ca. 399.6 eV, graphitic (or quaternary) N at 400.4 eV, and N-oxides (e.g., O = C‒N) at 401.4 eV [38,39], as illustrated in Figs. 3(d)−3(f). As detailed in Figs. 3, the HN-GQD sample possesses a high fraction of graphitic N (~72.6%) that mainly originates from the crystalline (CNOH)3 embedded within the HN-GQD sample, as identified with the XRD analysis. In contrast, the LN-GQD sample contains pyrrolic/pyridinic N (~53.7%), graphitic N (~20.0%), and N-oxides (~26.3%), whereas MN-GQD sample consists of pyrrolic/pyridinic N (~50.6%), graphitic N (~44.8%), and N-oxides (~4.6%). According to the XPS analysis, the molar ratio of urea to citric acid is a detrimental factor affecting the amidation level as well as the N-functionalities distribution within the GQDs nanostructure.

HR-TEM micrographs of all GQDs prepared by the IR-assisted pyrolysis of urea and citric acid are given in Figs. 4 (a)-(c). Homogeneous dispersion of all GQD samples can be identified in Figs. 4, where the single particles have formed a round-like shape with an average particle size of ~30 nm. Figs. 4(d)-(f) demonstrate FE-SEM images of LN-GQD, MN-GQD, and HN-GQD electrodes, respectively. As shown in Figs. 4, a large amount of GQDs deposited onto the surface of carbon fiber with an average diameter of 8 μm. The surface densities of LN-GQD, MN-GQD, and HN-GQD electrodes were also determined as 0.32, 0.29, and 0.30 mg cm−2, respectively; based on the increased electrode weight upon deposition of the GQD coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ESA of highly amidized gqd catalyst electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The CV potential cycling was conducted to examine the ESA of different GQD catalysts in 1 M H2SO4 under N2 atmosphere. Fig. 5 (a) depicts the cyclic voltammograms of MN-GQD catalyst electrodes at different scan rates (10, 30, 50, and 100 mV s −1) starting from the open circuit potential and varying within the range of 0 to 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The CV profiles of the MN-GQD electrode consist of three regions: (i) adsorption-desorption of hydrogen (0–0.4 V), (ii) double-layer formation (0.4–0.9 V), and (iii) oxidation–reduction of GQDs (0.9–1.0 V). The CV profiles for the MN-GQD electrode demonstrate a similar trend as of the Pt-based catalyst electrodes [40]. Based on the capacitive double-layer behavior within 0.4‒0.9 V vs. Ag/AgCl, it can be confirmed that highly amidized GQDs are electrochemically active for the formation of a double layer. This is attributed to an improved surface hydrophilicity due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the GQD surface.

To clarify the presence of H-adsorption/desorption pair, the CV measurement of GQD-coated CP electrode in H2SO4 at different scan rates under atmospheric environment (without any nitrogen or oxygen gas flow) was also performed. We observe that the pair of redox (i.e., adsorption-desorption of hydrogen) disappears within the potential region of 0‒0.4 V. This finding reveals that the pair of redox peaks (see Fig. 5) in the potential region of 0–0.4 V originates from the adsorption-desorption of hydrogen. The reversible reaction can be formulated as:
(R1) GQD* + H + + e – ↔ GQD*–Hads

Here, GQD* represents the active sites available for adsorbing hydrogen atoms and GQD*–Hads represents H-adsorbed intermediates. The GQD catalyst electrode provides a large number of active sites available for reversible adsorption/desorption of hydrogen atoms due to intrinsic nanoscale structure and nicely dispersed distribution within the CP substrate.

The ESA value, relative to the monolayer adsorption on the Pt nanoparticle, can be evaluated from Eq. (1) as formulated in Section 2.2. The ESA value as a function of scan rate for all GQD electrodes is illustrated in Fig. 5(b). According to Fig. 5(b), the ESA value remains relatively unchanged with increased scan rate indicating insignificant effect of ionic diffusion resistance on the reaction rate of the reversible adsorption/desorption of H atoms. To effectively reduce the mass transfer resistance, downsizing the GQD nanoparticles is an effective approach for increasing the ESA value at high rates. According to Fig. 5, the MN-GQD electrode offers the highest ESA value among the electrodes, where the ESA value reaches as high as 50.6 cm2 g −1 at 10 mV s −1. In comparison, the ESA values of LN-GQD and HN-GQD electrodes were 43.4 and 36.8 cm2 g −1 at 10 mV s −1, respectively. This sequence of ESA value reveals the importance of N substitution on the H-adsorption capability on the highly amidized GQD electrodes. Due to the coexistence of graphene-like and cyanuric acid crystals, HN-GQD catalyst exhibits a lower electrochemical activity toward H-adsorption/desorption as compared to other samples. Indeed, the presence of cyanuric acid adversely affects the performance and results in a partially inactive surface coverage.

Compared to the LN-GQD sample, MN-GQD electrode possesses higher amidation level (N/C: 33 at.%) along with more pyrridinic/pyrrolic N atoms. The critical influence of the N substitution within carbon materials in promoting the H-adsorption has already been demonstrated via the density functional theory (DFT) [41]. The DFT calculations have revealed that the substitutional N atoms improve the adsorption energy of hydrogen atoms on top of the neighboring C-atoms [42], as schematically illustrated in Fig. 6 . That is, the adsorption of H atom within N-doped GQDs is energetically favored when H atom is adsorbed on the C atom rather than on the N atom. Indeed, the C atoms, next to pyridinic N atoms and bonded to the H atoms, are protruded from the original surface of highly amidized GQDs where the C–H pair forms a covalent bond [43]. Upon the adsorption of H atoms, the configuration of the C atom in the GQDs transforms from sp2 into sp3-hybridized configurations. Subsequently, the N-substitution facilitates the transportation of H atoms into in the carbon lattice [44]. The weak adsorption sites are normally associated with the basal planes consisting of the hexagonal arrangement of carbon atoms, while the high energy sites appearing at the edges, are often saturated with hydrogen heteroatoms [45]. Among all the samples, MN-GQD electrode, contains a large number of substitutional N atoms (e.g., pyridinic N) at the edge of carbon network; thus provides more electroactive sites for adsorbing protons and storing H atoms in the highly amidized GQDs. This finding demonstrates that highly amidized GQDs are promising nanomaterials for electrochemical hydrogen storage.

It is also important to explore the degradation behavior of highly amidized GQD electrodes. Therefore, electrochemically active sites as well as the surface coverage must be quantified upon long-duration cycling. The electrochemical durability is also crucial in evaluating the reversibility of H-adsorption/desorption and the anti-corrosion capability in the interfacial region formed between the GQDs and CP substrate. Fig. 5(c) shows the cyclic voltammograms of HN-GQD electrode at 50 mV s −1, which confirms its excellent durability within 400 cycles. The variation of the ESA value with cycle number is depicted in Fig. 5(d). After long-duration cycling, all GQD electrodes maintain 76‒78% ESA retention as compared to the corresponding ESA value assessed at the end of first cycle. The slight decrease in the ESA value is presumably the result of the Nafion fouling within the acidic electrolytes. As expected, the MN-GQD electrode still maintains highest electroactive surface area among all the electrode configurations and exhibits excellent durability toward H-adsorption/desorption in extremely acidic electrolytes. Accordingly, highly amidized GQDs are promising non-metallic nanomaterials with low cost, superior durability, and remarkable catalytic activity within acidic electrolytes that can replace the precious-metal catalysts commonly used in electrochemical systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR activity of highly amidized gqd catalyst electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
To inspect the ORR activity, the LSV measurements were conducted on the GQD electrodes using 1 M sulfuric acid as the electrolyte at various sweep rates (10, 30, 50, and 100 mV s −1) with the O2 gas flowing at 100 ml min−1, as shown in Figs. 7 (a)−7(c). The maximal current within the entire potential window at 100 mV s −1 was achieved for the MN-GQD electrode configuration (2.12 A g −1) followed by the LN-GQD (1.70 A g −1) and HN-GQD (1.35 A g −1) electrodes. This sequence is also in good agreement with the corresponding onset potentials. As shown in Fig. 7, the MN-GQD catalyst electrode provides the highest ORR activity, followed by the LN-GQD and HN-GQD samples, demonstrating a similar trend observed for the sequence of ESA values. The reaction mechanism of ORR on highly amidized GQDs in the presence of acidic electrolyte can be formulated as [18,45]:
(R2) GQD* + O2 → GQD*−O2(ads)
(R3) GQD*−O2(ads) + 4 H + + 4 e − → GQD* + 2 H2O
(R4) GQD*−O2(ads) + 2 H + + 2 e − → GQD*−H2O2(ads)
(R5) GQD*−H2O2(ads) + 2 H + + 2 e − → GQD* + 2 H2O

Herein the ORR catalytic cycle consists of (i) chemsorption of O2 (R2), (ii) four-electron (R3), and (iii) two-electron charge transfer steps (R4 and R5). It is well known that a sluggish ORR on the Pt catalyst electrode, either through a four-electron process (i.e., R3) or a less efficient two-step two-electron pathway (i.e., R4 and R5) [46] is the rate-determining step limiting the energy conversion efficiency. Moreover, the Pt-based electrode suffers fromits susceptibility to time-dependent drift and CO deactivation [46,47]. As observed from the LSV measurements, highly amidized GQDs serve as a metal-free catalyst toward ORR, capable of chemically catalyzing O-adsorbed species and thereby forming water. The CO poisoning on the GQD catalysts can be alleviated due to the presence of O-rich functionalities attached to the carbon nanostructure. Indeed, the hydroxyl groups (–OH) sites effectively oxidize the CO into CO2 [40] mitigating commonly observed poisoing effect induced by the existence of CO within the Pt-based catalytic systems.

The improved ORR activity achieved via the MN-GQD catalytic electrode is attributed to the fact that N-induced charge-transfer from adjacent carbon atoms decreases the ORR potential, while the parallel diatomic adsorption effectively weakens the O‒O bonding, and subsequently enhances the ORR at highly amidized GQD electrodes [48]. The substitutional-N center geometry mostly improves the O2 adsorption onto the adjacent C atoms and breaks the O − O bond fasciliating the reduciton of O2 via a four-electron reduction pathway, i.e., R2 and R3 [49]. Additionally, N dopants at the edge (e.g., pyridinic N) or basal planes (e.g., graphitic N) promotes the electron-accepting ability, inducing a net positive charge on the adjacent carbon atoms in highly amidized GQDs; and subsequently attracts the electrons liberated through the catalytic electrodes. This sequential process ultimately facilitates the ORR activity [45].

Figs. 7(d)−7(f) depict the LSV profiles on the GQD electrodes in 0.1 M KOH at different sweep rates (10, 30, 50, and 100 mV s −1) with the O2 gas flowing at volumetric flow rate of 100 ml min−1. As illustrated in Figs. 7, highly amidized GQDs display superior catalytic ORR activity within alkaline electrolytes saturated in O2. For the MN-GQD catalytic electrode, an onset potential appears at ca. −0.17 V with a well-defined reduction peak at ca. −0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The ORR peak current of MN-GQD catalyst electrode attains as high as ca. 15 A g −1 at 100 mV s −1, which is 1.3 and 2.1 times higher than that of LN-GQD and HN-GQD samples, respectively. It is generally recognized that no obvious ORR electrocatalytic activity was found in the ORR tests on the pure graphene and N-free GQD/graphene performed in O2-saturated KOH solutions [50]. This finding reveals that the ORR catalytic activity is significantly enhanced by the N dopants into the GQDs.

Previous study has reported that pure Pt and Pt-Sn catalyst showed the ORR activities: ca. 4 and 16 A g −1 in 1 M H2SO4 at 30 mV s −1 [51]. The magnitudes of Pt and Pt-Sn catalysts are higher than that of GQD/CP catalyst electrodes in H2SO4 electrolyte; however, both catalyst electrodes exhibit an identical turn-on voltage. It believes that the ORR activity of GQD-coated CP electrodes can be significantly improved if we reduce the particle size and improve the electrical conductivity of highly amidized GQDs. In contrast, the MN-GQD catalyst electrode in 0.1 M KOH delivers a maximal ORR current (ca. 21 A g −1) at 30 mV s −1 (see Fig. 7(e)), higher that than of Pt and Pt-Sn catalyst. This reveals that highly-amidized GQDs with an appropriate N-dopant (e.g., pyridinic N) display a feasibility to replace costly precious metal catalysts, e.g., Pt, Pd, and so on.

The reaction steps for the ORR on highly amidized GQDs in KOH electrolyte can be expressed as the follolwing sequence of reactions [45]:
(R6) GQD* + O2 → GQD*−O2(ads)
(R7) GQD*−O2(ads) + 2 H2O + 4 e − → GQD* + 4 OH−
(R8) GQD*−O2(ads) + 2 H2O + 2 e − → GQD*‒HO2 − (ads) + OH−
(R9) GQD*‒HO2 − (ads) + H2O + 2 e − → GQD* + 3 OH−

Here, the ORR catalytic cycle is composed of (i) chemsorption of O2 (R6), (ii) four-electron (R7), and (iii) two-electron charge transfer steps (R8 and R9). Theoretical studies have been performed to understand the remarkable ORR activity observed on the N-doped carbon-based materials. Theoretically, the four-electron ORR pathway (i.e., R7) can be achieved with an adequate binding energy of the oxygen atom on the multiple N-doped graphene centers [52]. The carbon atoms adjacent to the pyridinic N with Lewis basicity play a critical role serving as the active sites for adsorbing the oxygen molecules and subsequently initiating the ORR cycle (i.e., R6) [18]. Meanwhile, pyridinic N sites (nitrogen atoms with lone pair electrons) promote the ORR activity due to delocalization of the π-electron from pyridinic N atoms [53]. The doped nitrogen with O = C‒N edge structure tend to catalyze the ORR in a two-electron pathway (i.e., R8 and R9) [54].

It is generally recognized that catalyzed ORR is a multi-step reaction [45]. Therefore, the rate of the rate-determining step is more important than the overall reaction rate. Previous studies have pointed out 4-electron pathway dominates the ORR multiple reaction steps, analyzed by rotating ring-disk electrode. The number of electrons transferred per oxygen molecule was extracted from the Koutecky-Levich plots (K-L) equation. The K-L analysis has shown that the electron number obtained is 3.86‒3.88 and 3.9 for N-doped graphene sheets [10] and N-doped GQDs [16], calculated from the K-L equation. This finding is consistent with a four electron process that have been reported for other N-doped carbon materials [45,55]. Therefore, similar catalytic mechanisms could also apply to highly amidized GQDs investigated in the present study.

One schematic diagram descrbing the four-electron pathway on low and high amidation level GQD catalysts is illustrated in Fig. 8 . Since MN-GQD catalyst contains the highest fraction of pyridinic N atoms with ultralow N-oxide groups, the N-edge substitutional structure enhances the oxygen adsorption and promotes the first electron transfer step along with the selectivity toward the four-electron transfer mechanism (i.e., R7), rather than the two-electron (i.e., R8 and R9), reduction pathway. This pathway is favorable for an improved ORR activity on the MN-GQD catalytic electrode. In contrast, the LN-GQD electrode contains a low amidation level with high fraction of pyridinic N atoms (53.7%) and N-oxide groups (26.3%), leading to two parallel pathways for the four-electron (i.e., R7) and two-electron (i.e., R8 and R9) reduction routes. Since the two-electron pathway contains two-step reduction reaction pathway, it is unfavorable for enhancing the ORR activity. Therefore, the interplay between the increased content of pyridinic N and the ORR performance on the active sites can be tailored for enhancing the ORR catalytic acitivy on highly amidized GQD electrodes. The present work provides the underlying details for evaluating the ORR activity in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes.

Typical Tafel plots for the ORR activity on different catalyst electrodes were analyzed by a LSV measurement under saturated oxygen atmosphere at 10 mV s −1. The Tafel plots are depicted in Figure S1 to further examine the reaction kinetics of GQD catalysts in H2SO4 and KOH within the overpotential. The Tafel plots can be formulated as a linear form, according to the alternative form of Butler-Volmer [56,57]:
(R10)
log
i = log
i 0
+
(
α n F
2.303 R T
)
η
where η is the overpotential, i 0 is the exchange current, α is the anodic transfer coefficient, n is the Faraday's constant, R is the universal gas constant, and T is the operating temperature. Based on the onset of LSV polarization curves, the Tafel slope and i 0 value are thus determined from the slope and intercept of linear plots, by extrapolating the linear region of Tafel plots [58]. The Tafel slopes for MN-GQD, LN-GQD, and HN-GQD catalyst electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 are 382, 745, and 737 mV dec−1, respectively, whereas the Tafel slopes in 0.1 M KOH are 105, 120, and 135 mV dec−1 for MN-GQD, LN-GQD, and HN-GQD electrodes, respectively. It is worth noting that the apparent Tafel slopes remain nearly the same trend at 10, 30, and 50 mV s −1. Again, the MN-GQD catalyst electrode exhibits the lowest Tafel slope among the electrodes, indicating the superior catalytic activity toward the ORR.

Highly amidized GQD catalysts were synthesized through an efficient IR-assisted method of pyrolyzing citric acid and urea at 250 °C. The as-prepared GQDs with an average particle size of 30 nm contained a high amidation level (N/C atomic ratio: 23‒46 at.%) with a large number of O-rich functionalities. The highly amidized GQD catalytic electrodes enabled ultra-high electrochemically active surface area with an ESA value reaching 5.06 cm2 g −1 at 10 mV s −1. The enhanced electrochemical H-adsorption capability is due to the GQDs containing a large number of substitutional N atoms (e.g. pyridinic N) at the edge of carbon network, providing additional electroactive sites for adsorbing proton and storing H atoms in the highly amidized GQDs. Therefore, highly amidized GQDs are also promising nanomaterials for the electrochemical hydrogen storage.

The highly amidized GQD catalytic electrodes displayed remarkable ORR capability in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes. The GQD electrode demonstrated an onset potential appearing at ca. −0.17 V with a well-defined reduction peak at ca. −0.35 V vs. Ag/AgCl in KOH electrolyte. The ORR catalytic activity in both electrolytes was significantly enhanced by the N dopants into the GQDs. Two ORR mechanisms were proposed to elaborate the sequential reaction mechanism in acid and base electrolytes, including chemsorption of O2, four-electron, and two-electron charge transfer steps. Experimental results revealed that highly amidized GQD catalyst contains extremely high fraction of pyridinic N atoms with ultralow N-oxide groups, where the N-edge substitutional structure effectively facilitates the oxygen adsorption, first electron transfer, and selectivity toward the four-electron reduction route, rather than the two-electron reduction pathway. This reduction route is favorable for an improved ORR activity on the GQD catalytic electrodes. Therefore, the development of highly amidized GQD catalysts paves the pathway for synthesizing non-metallic and inexpensive catalysts with remarkable attributes including superior durability, selectivity, and high catalytic activity.

The authors are very grateful for the financial support the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108–2221-E-155–036-MY3). Prof. Gu also thanks for the support from the Talents Introduction Program of Xiamen University of Technology (YKJ19018R) and Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in Fujian Province University (IRTSTFJ).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2020.137009.
